text: Outage
Downtime
Migrate
Back up
Escalate
Prioritize
Scenario
Worst-case
If there is an outage, we will escalate it and back up the data.
If the test passes, we will migrate and prioritize the database first.
In the worst-case scenario, if we had less downtime, we would finish sooner.
If the server goes down, we will escalate the problem.
If the test passes, we will migrate on Monday.
If we had a bigger budget, we would migrate to the cloud.
If I were you, I would tell the directors now.
If the test passes, we will migrate, but if we had more time, we would test it twice.
Daniela, what is the plan if the test goes well this week?
If the test passes, we will migrate the data on Monday and prioritize the database first.
And if something breaks during the migration?
If there is an outage, we will roll back to the old system, and I will escalate it to the vendor.
Good. Honestly, if we had more downtime allowed, I would do it all in one weekend.
True, but we cannot. If I were you, I would plan for the worst-case scenario from the start.
Agreed. So if we back up everything first, we will be safe even if it fails.
Exactly. I will tell the directors today, so if anything goes wrong, they will already know the plan.
If there is an outage, I will escalate it to the vendor.
If we had more time, we would test it twice.
If I were you, I would plan for the worst-case scenario.
If we back up first, we will prioritize the database.

Hi Daniela, it is Sarah, your English coach. Today, listen to one short voice note about conditionals, the way we connect a condition with a result. There are two kinds you need at work. The first conditional is for real, likely situations. The form is, if plus present simple, then will plus the base verb. For example, if the server goes down, we will escalate the problem to the team. This is realistic, it can really happen. The second conditional is for imagined or unlikely situations. The form is, if plus past simple, then would plus the base verb. For example, if we had a bigger budget, we would migrate everything to the cloud. This is hypothetical, just an idea. Here is the key. In the second conditional we use the past form, but it is not really about the past. It is an imagined now. So remember, first conditional for real plans, will, and second conditional for hypothetical scenarios, would. Try it. Think about one real risk at work and one dream project, and make a sentence for each. Talk soon.

Hello Daniela, this is Marcus. I am leaving you a quick voicemail about the migration plan and the worst-case scenario. So, here is my thinking. If the test goes well this week, we will start the migration on Monday, that is the plan. But we need a backup plan too. If something breaks during the migration, we will roll back to the old system immediately, and I will escalate it to you and to the vendor. Now, honestly, if we had more downtime allowed, I would do the whole thing in one weekend, but we do not, so we have to do it in stages. And if I were you, I would tell the directors about the risk now, before we start, not after. One more thing. If the new platform did not depend on the old database, this would be so much easier, but it does, so we will prioritize the data first. Call me back when you can, and we will finalize the plan. Talk soon, Daniela.
